title: End of module: The Normal Distribution

choices: A continuous, bell-shaped distribution symmetric about the mean $\mu$, with spread controlled by $\sigma$
A continuous distribution that is always skewed to the right
A distribution defined only for integer values of $X$
questions.explanation: $N(\mu,\sigma^2)$ is the continuous normal distribution: a symmetric bell curve centred on the mean $\mu$, with $\sigma$ (the standard deviation) controlling how spread out it is. It is not discrete and is not skewed — it is perfectly symmetric.

questions.text: $X \sim N(40, 6^2)$. Find the standardised value $z$ for $x = 46$.
$X \sim N(50, 8^2)$. Find $P(X < 63)$, giving your answer to 4 decimal places.
questions.explanation: Standardise: $z = \dfrac{63-50}{8} = 1.625$. Then $P(X<63) = \Phi(1.625) \approx 0.9479$ (standard normal CDF, e.g. from a calculator's normal CDF function or interpolated tables: $\Phi(1.6) \approx 0.9452$, $\Phi(1.65)\approx0.9505$, giving $\approx 0.9479$ at $z=1.625$).

questions.text: $X \sim N(50, 8^2)$. Find $P(X > 44)$, giving your answer to 4 decimal places.
questions.explanation: Standardise: $z = \dfrac{44-50}{8} = -0.75$. Then $P(X>44) = 1-\Phi(-0.75) = \Phi(0.75) \approx 0.7734$ (since $P(X>44)$ mirrors $P(X<56)$ by symmetry, and $\Phi(0.75)\approx0.7734$ from standard tables).

questions.text: $X \sim N(50, 8^2)$. Find $P(42 < X < 58)$, giving your answer to 4 decimal places.
questions.explanation: Standardise both ends: $z_1 = \dfrac{42-50}{8} = -1$, $z_2 = \dfrac{58-50}{8} = 1$. Then $P(42<X<58) = \Phi(1)-\Phi(-1) = 0.8413 - 0.1587 = 0.6826$ (this is exactly the '68' in the 68-95-99.7 rule: about 68% of a normal distribution lies within 1 standard deviation of the mean).

questions.text: $\Phi(z)$ denotes the standard normal cumulative distribution function. Which of these best describes $\Phi(z)$?
choices: $P(Z = z)$, the probability that $Z$ equals exactly $z$
$P(Z < z)$, the probability that a standard normal variable is less than $z$
$P(Z > z)$, the probability that a standard normal variable is greater than $z$
questions.explanation: $\Phi(z) = P(Z<z)$: the area under the standard normal curve to the left of $z$. Since the normal distribution is continuous, $P(Z=z)=0$ for any single value, so it can't be option 1.

questions.text: Exam scores are $X \sim N(70, 10^2)$. The top 5% of students get a distinguished pass. Find the minimum score $a$ needed, to 1 decimal place.
questions.explanation: "Top 5%" means $P(X>a) = 0.05$, so $P(X<a) = 0.95$. The $z$-value with $\Phi(z) = 0.95$ is $z = 1.6449$ (standard inverse-normal value). Then $a = \mu + z\sigma = 70 + 1.6449 \times 10 = 86.449 \approx 86.4$.

questions.text: Which condition makes a normal approximation to $X \sim B(n,p)$ appropriate?
choices: $n$ is small and $p$ is close to $0.5$
$np > 5$ and $n(1-p) > 5$ (large $n$, $p$ not too close to $0$ or $1$)
questions.explanation: The normal approximation to the binomial works well when $n$ is large enough that both $np>5$ and $n(1-p)>5$, so the binomial's shape is already close to a symmetric bell — extreme $p$ values (near 0 or 1) or small $n$ make the approximation poor.

questions.text: $X \sim B(80, 0.5)$. Using a normal approximation with a continuity correction, estimate $P(X \ge 45)$, giving your answer to 4 decimal places.
questions.explanation: Match moments: $\mu = np = 40$, $\sigma^2 = np(1-p) = 20$, so $\sigma = \sqrt{20} \approx 4.472$. Apply the continuity correction: $P(X \ge 45) \approx P(Y > 44.5)$. Standardise: $z = \dfrac{44.5-40}{\sqrt{20}} \approx 1.006$. Then $P(Y>44.5) = 1-\Phi(1.006) \approx 1 - 0.8428 = 0.1572$.

questions.text: For $X \sim B(n,p)$ approximated by $Y \sim N(np, np(1-p))$, which continuity-corrected expression correctly estimates $P(X \le k)$?
questions.explanation: Because $X$ is discrete and $Y$ is continuous, we widen the boundary outward by $0.5$ in the direction that includes $k$: $P(X\le k)$ (which includes the whole of $k$) becomes $P(Y < k+0.5)$.
